Name: spectral_latent_trajectory_optimization
Title: Training-Free Latent Trajectory Optimization via Spectral Subspace Projection
Short Hypothesis: Restricting test-time optimization updates to a low-rank spectral subspace identified by PCA can achieve efficient and stable inference while adhering to strict latency constraints.
Related Work: Existing work focuses on spectral quantization (SQR), geometric interference cancellation, and PCA-based filtering for activation geometry. This proposal differs by explicitly targeting latent trajectory optimization, leveraging spectral subspaces to enforce latency budgets without retraining or heuristic control mechanisms.
Abstract: Test-time optimization has emerged as a powerful tool for improving model performance without retraining. However, existing methods often suffer from unpredictable latency and high computational overhead. We propose Spectral Latent Trajectory Optimization (SLTO), a training-free framework that restricts optimization updates to a low-rank spectral subspace identified via PCA. By performing a single-pass PCA on initial layer activations, we identify dominant spectral directions that govern latent refinement. Subsequent updates are projected onto this subspace, ensuring strict computational budgets while preserving optimization stability. We theoretically ground SLTO in PAC-Bayes bounds, showing that low-rank projections capture gradient-aligned signal efficiently. Empirically, SLTO matches or exceeds full-space baselines on reasoning and generation tasks while reducing perturbation overhead by over 70% and enforcing deterministic latency bounds. This work bridges optimization theory and practical inference, offering a reproducible path to efficient test-time scaling.
Experiments: Task 1: Mathematical Reasoning. Evaluate SLTO on GSM8K and MATH datasets, comparing accuracy and latency against full-space optimization baselines.
Task 2: Code Generation. Test on HumanEval, measuring pass rates and inference speed under strict latency constraints.
Task 3: Robustness to Distribution Shifts. Assess performance on WILDS benchmarks, quantifying accuracy retention and compute efficiency.
Metrics: Accuracy, latency (ms), perturbation cost (FLOPs), and spectral subspace alignment stability.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: Low-rank projections may oversimplify the optimization landscape, leading to suboptimal updates.
Risk 2: Single-pass PCA may fail to capture dynamic spectral properties during inference.
Limitation: Requires initial activation computation, introducing a small upfront cost.
Limitation: Performance may degrade on tasks requiring high-dimensional latent refinements.
